1: If a man steals (ganov yignov) an ox (shor) or a sheep (seh): The doubled infinitive absolute ganov yignov (steal he steals) emphasizes the definite act. The asymmetric restitution — five oxen for one stolen ox, four sheep for one stolen sheep — reflects their relative economic value in ancient Israel: an ox was a working animal critical for plowing and food production; a sheep was smaller capital. The fivefold restitution for ox is the highest in the Code; theft of an ox effectively deprives a family of its livelihood.
2: If a thief is found breaking in (bamachtereth) and is struck and dies: Machtereth = the act of tunneling through a wall (from chatar, to dig/burrow); breaking into a house at night through the wall rather than the door. The law permits lethal self-defense at night — the darkness makes the intruder's intent uncertain. There shall be no blood guilt (ein lo damim): Damim (bloods, plural) = blood guilt/liability; the homeowner is not guilty of murder because he acted in self-defense under conditions of uncertainty.
3: But if the sun has risen on him (im zarach hashemesh alav), there shall be blood guilt: Daylight changes the moral calculus — in the daytime the homeowner can see the intruder and assess the threat; lethal force is not presumptively necessary. The distinction is one of the oldest graduated lethality-in-self-defense principles in legal history. The thief shall make restitution (shalleim y'shalleim): Shalom = peace/wholeness; shilleim = to make whole/restore; the doubled form is the standard legal intensifier for binding obligation.
4: If the stolen animal is found alive in his possession: The living animal in hand is evidence; restitution is twofold (double, not fivefold) because the animal has not been slaughtered or sold — the theft was caught before its consequences were fully enacted. The principle: restitution scales with the harm actually done, not just the crime attempted.
5: If a man causes a field or vineyard to be grazed over (ba'er) by letting his livestock loose: Ba'ar = to consume by grazing or burning; the root covers both animals browsing and fire consuming. The principle of negligent damage: the owner is responsible for what his livestock does on others' land. He shall make restitution from the best of his own field: The quality standard — meitav (the best/finest) — ensures the offended party receives above-replacement value, incentivizing careful livestock management.
6: If fire breaks out and catches in thorns (qotzim): Qotzim = thorns/briers; the dry thorns of the Palestinian summer are classic fire fuel. The law addresses negligent fire spread — someone burning a field who allows the fire to escape into a neighbor's grain or standing crops. Responsibility follows the ignition source, regardless of wind direction.
7: If a man gives his neighbor money or goods to keep (lishmor): Shamar = to guard/keep watch; the safekeeping/bailment relationship (mishmeret = guardianship). The stolen goods from a trusted keeper entail a different liability than open theft; the keeper must prove innocence before elohim (the judges or the divine tribunal).
8: If the thief is not found, the owner of the house shall come near to God (ha-Elohim): Ha-Elohim in the judicial sense = divine tribunal or judges acting as divine representatives. The repeated el ha-Elohim in this section (vv.8,9) may refer to a formal oath-taking before God or before human judges who represent divine authority. Disputed property cases go to the divine tribunal — human courts operate under divine oversight.
9: For every breach of trust (kol-davar pesha') — for ox, donkey, sheep, garment, or any lost thing: Pesha' = transgression/rebellion (the strongest Hebrew word for sin — deliberate violation of relationship); here used for breach of trust/fiduciary duty. Both parties bring their case before ha-Elohim; the guilty party pays double. The doubling principle (mishneh) is the base penalty in disputed cases.
10: If a man gives his neighbor a donkey or ox or sheep or any beast to keep: The bailment law continues; the keeper who watches an animal that dies or is injured without witnesses (ein re'eh) swears an oath (shevuat YHWH) that he did not lay his hand on it — the oath transfers the case from human to divine adjudication.
11: An oath by YHWH shall be between them both: Shevuat YHWH = YHWH's oath/oath-by-YHWH; the divine name functions as the ultimate guarantor of truthfulness. The owner accepts the oath and the keeper is absolved — no restitution. If the animal is stolen (after the oath), the keeper must make restitution; the oath covered negligence, not theft.
12: If it was stolen from him, he shall make restitution to its owner: The keeper who allows theft bears liability — the obligation of safekeeping includes protecting from theft. But if torn by wild animals (tarof yitaref), the keeper brings the evidence (the torn carcass) and is absolved; he swears he did not eat it (lo yeshalleim).
13: If a wild beast has torn it (tarof yitaref), let him bring it as evidence: The doubled tarof yitaref (torn it was torn) signals the severity and definiteness of the loss. The torn carcass (ha-terefa) is brought as legal evidence of force majeure — the keeper cannot be held liable for acts of predation he could not prevent.
14: If a man borrows (yish'al) anything from his neighbor and it is injured or dies: Sha'al = to ask/borrow/request; the loan relationship (she'elah) creates the strictest liability — the borrower who uses another's animal bears full responsibility if it is harmed while in his use. The owner not being with it, he shall make full restitution: The owner's absence is the key legal fact; if the owner was present (imo, with him), the liability is reduced because the owner participated in the use.
15: If the owner was with it, he shall not make restitution: The owner's presence (imo = with him) during the use means he bears joint risk and has no claim. If it was hired, it came for its hire: The hired animal's risk is priced into the rental fee (sekaro ba ba'avuro = its wage came in its wages) — the renter has already paid for the assumed risk.
16: If a man seduces (yefateh) a virgin who is not betrothed: Pathah = to entice/persuade/seduce (also used of prophetic inspiration gone wrong — Jer 20:7); the consensual seduction of an unbetrothed virgin. The man must pay the mohar (bride price) and marry her — the legal remedy prioritizes the woman's long-term social protection over punitive measures. If her father refuses the marriage, the man still pays the full mohar.
17: If her father utterly refuses to give her to him: The father's veto (refusal to give = me'en yema'en) is legally binding; the daughter cannot be forced to marry her seducer if her father objects. He shall pay money equal to the bride price for virgins (kemoharn'betulot): Mohar = bride price (the payment from groom to bride's family that transferred legal responsibility and provided the bride with economic security); betulot = virgins.
18: You shall not permit a sorceress (m'kashapha) to live: Kashaph = to practice sorcery/witchcraft; the participle form identifies the person by ongoing practice. The feminine form (m'kashapha) is notable — sorcery was predominantly associated with women in ancient Near Eastern contexts. The capital penalty reflects the seriousness of the offense: the sorcerer claims to mediate between the human and divine realms outside YHWH's authorization.
19: Whoever lies with an animal (shochev im behemah) shall be put to death: Shachav = to lie/sleep with (the standard sexual euphemism); behemah = beast/livestock. The death penalty covers both human and animal (Lev 20:15-16). The violation of the created order (Gen 1's separation of species) is the underlying rationale; sexual congress across species categories inverts the creation mandate.
20: Whoever sacrifices (zovach) to any god (la-elohim acherim) other than YHWH alone shall be devoted to destruction (yochoram): Zovach = to slaughter as sacrifice; cherem (related to yochoram) = the devoted ban — complete destruction as YHWH's property, the highest and most irrevocable sanction in Israel's law. Sacrificing to other gods is not merely religious deviance but treason against the suzerain of the covenant.
21: You shall not wrong (toneh) a sojourner (ger): Yanah = to oppress/wrong/maltreat; ger = resident alien/sojourner (from gur, to sojourn/live as a guest). The ger is the vulnerable outsider living within Israelite society without tribal protection. The grounding for the prohibition is covenantal memory: you were gerim in Egypt (ki gerim heyitem b'eretz mitzrayim). Israel's experience of oppression creates the ethical obligation to protect those in the same social position.
22: You shall not afflict (t'anun) any widow (almanah) or fatherless child (yatom): Anah = to afflict/humble/oppress; almanah = widow (a woman without a male protector); yatom = orphan (literally fatherless — without a father's economic protection). The almanah-yatom-ger triad recurs throughout the law codes and prophets as the paradigm of the vulnerable for whom YHWH himself acts as protector. Their mistreatment triggers direct divine response.
23: If you do afflict them and they cry out to me (veitz'aku elai): Za'aq = to cry out in distress (the same verb as 2:23 — the slaves' cry); YHWH responds to the cry of the powerless with wrath (charah appi). The consequence is in kind: their widows and orphaned children (almnoteihem yih'yu almanot, u'vneichem yetomim) mirror the oppressor's victims. The covenant law has a built-in retributive logic: do to others what was done to you in Egypt, and I will do to your families what Egypt did to you.
24: If you lend money (halveh) to any of my people who is poor (aniy): Lavah = to lend/borrow (the borrower is servant to the lender — Prov 22:7 uses the same root). You shall not be like a moneylender (k'nosheh) to him, and you shall not exact interest (neshek): Nosheh = one who drives/exacts; neshek = interest (literally a bite — from nashak, to bite; interest that bites into principal). The anti-usury law is rooted in covenantal solidarity: the poor Israelite is ach (brother), not a business opportunity.
25: If you ever take your neighbor's cloak (salmah) in pledge (chabol tachbelenu): Salmah = garment/cloak (the outer garment that doubled as a blanket); chabol = to take a pledge/hold as security (from chebl = cord/band). The poor man's only covering — his cloak — cannot be permanently retained as collateral. You shall return it to him before the sun goes down: The sunset deadline (ad-ba hashemesh) transforms property law into a humanitarian obligation; the cloak is not merely collateral but the man's bedding.
26: For that is his only covering (ki hi kesuto levadah): Levadah = alone/only; kesut = covering. It is his cloak for his body; in what else shall he sleep? The rhetorical question (b'mah yishkav?) is an appeal to empathy embedded in the law itself. And if he cries to me, I will hear, for I am compassionate (chanun ani): Chanun = gracious/compassionate (one of the divine attributes of Exod 34:6-7); the same word used of YHWH's character at Sinai. The covenant law is not just a social contract but a reflection of the divine character.
27: You shall not revile God (elohim lo teqallel) nor curse a ruler (nasi) of your people: Qalal = to make light, curse, hold in contempt; elohim here may refer to God directly or to judges/rulers as divine representatives (the word is ambiguous in this context; the LXX reads 'gods' = judges). Nasi = leader/prince (literally 'one lifted up' from nasa'). Both the divine order and human governance are protected from the same verb — profaning authority at any level dishonors the structure YHWH has established.
28: You shall not delay to offer from the fullness of your harvest (male'atcha) and from the outflow of your presses (dem'acha): Male'ah = fullness/abundance; dema' = the first pressing/overflow of pressed grapes or oil. The firstborn of your sons you shall give to me: The firstborn son (bechor) dedication principle (cf. 13:2); the male is given to YHWH — later redeemed by the Levites (Num 3) or by the five-shekel redemption price.
29: You shall do the same with your oxen and with your sheep: The parallel between firstborn sons and firstborn animals in vv.28-30 frames the entire created order as YHWH's — humans and animals both subject to the firstborn principle. Seven days it shall be with its mother (shiv'at yamim yihyeh im-imo): The seven-day nursing period before separation echoes the creation week; the animal is given time to bond before being dedicated — a compassionate interval even in the consecration law.
30: On the eighth day (bayom hash'mini) you shall give it to me: The eighth day = the day after the complete week, the day of new beginnings. Circumcision also occurs on the eighth day (Gen 17:12). The pattern of seven-plus-one (fullness + new beginning) structures both animal dedication and human covenant initiation.
31: You shall be consecrated people to me (anshei qodesh tih'yun li): Anshei qodesh = men of holiness/consecrated people; the dietary conclusion of chapter 22 is framed as a holiness identity-statement, not merely a health rule. Therefore you shall not eat any flesh that is torn by beasts in the field (treifah): Treifah (from taraf, to tear/rend) = an animal killed by a predator in the field; this is the first explicit kashrut (dietary law) category outside the Leviticus system. You shall throw it to the dogs (la-kelev tashlichun oto): The torn carcass goes to scavengers — Israel is holy; scavenger food belongs to scavengers.
